title: Can Europe Thrive Again? Assessing Global Leadership
content:
The fate of Europe has recently turned into a geopolitical Rorschach test for outside observers. For some American politicians, the continent represents a bastion of liberal decadence in terminal decline, prompting calls to abandon European alliances. Yet for many others, Brussels represents the world’s last hope for steady, rules-based global leadership. Despite this optimism, many Europeans themselves look jealously toward the United States, citing its higher rates of economic growth and world-beating technology companies. They frequently gripe about the European Union’s glacial pace of reform and the divergent interests of its member states.

David Marsh explores these anxieties in his book, Can Europe Survive, crafting a narrative history of key moments in Europe’s recent past. The book paints an overall picture of a continent buffeted by a cyclone of internal and external pressures, from Brexit to the European debt crisis. However, while the title poses an existential question, the real issue is not survival but whether Europe can act forcefully on the world stage. Today, Europe faces daunting challenges, including a volatile U.S. administration and a mercantilist China that threatens European industry. The ultimate test is whether Europe can move at the speed of relevancy to boost its geoeconomic and geopolitical vitality.

Despite these hurdles, there is ample evidence of European progress toward greater cohesion and self-defense over the last five years. The COVID-19 pandemic catalyzed the EU to jointly borrow funds, crossing a previously bright line of pooling debt. Furthermore, Russia’s invasion of Ukraine saw Europe rally to provide massive financial and military aid, surprisingly outpacing U.S. contributions. Key leaders across the continent remain committed to breaking down internal barriers and protecting the bloc’s economic interests. Ultimately, the European project continues to prove Jean Monnet’s famous dictum that Europe will be forged in crisis.
